tex:
**[2024 May/June, 0580/31, Question 1]**

(a) The cost of a cinema ticket is \$6.30.
\newline

(i) (b) Work out the total cost of 4 tickets.
\newline

(c) The cinema sells 10 tickets for the price of 9 tickets.
\newline
A group of 10 people go to the cinema.
\newline
Work out how much each person must pay.
\newline

(d) The cinema has 650 seats.
\newline

(i) (e) There are 26 seats in each row.
\newline
Work out the number of rows.
\newline

(f) 84\% of the seats are occupied.
\newline
Work out how many seats are not occupied.
\newline

(g) Suki buys a bag of popcorn and a bottle of water.
\newline
A bag of popcorn costs twice as much as a bottle of water.
\newline
She receives \$3.55 change from \$10.
\newline
Work out the cost of a bag of popcorn and the cost of a bottle of water.
\newline

Bag of popcorn 
Bottle of water 

(h) (i) (i) A film lasts for 155 minutes.
\newline
Write 155 minutes in hours and minutes.
\newline

(j) Another film starts at 14 45 and lasts for 2 hours and 20 minutes.
\newline
Work out the time that this film finishes.
\newline

(k) The table shows the hours that Trevor works in the cinema each week.
\newline

Sunday

Trevor is paid \$11.50 per hour for the time he works before 18 00.
\newline
He is paid 30\% more for the time he works after 18 00.
\newline
Work out how much Trevor is paid each week.
\newline

_rawBody:
\noindent\textbf{[2024 May/June, 0580/31, Question 1]}\par

\begin{parts}
\item The cost of a cinema ticket is \$6.30.

\begin{subparts}
\item Work out the total cost of 4 tickets.

\AnswerLine[][\$]
\Marks{1}

\item The cinema sells 10 tickets for the price of 9 tickets.
A group of 10 people go to the cinema.
Work out how much each person must pay.

\AnswerLine[][\$]
\Marks{2}

\end{subparts}

\item The cinema has 650 seats.

\begin{subparts}
\item There are 26 seats in each row.
Work out the number of rows.

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{1}

\item 84\% of the seats are occupied.
Work out how many seats are not occupied.

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{2}

\end{subparts}

\item Suki buys a bag of popcorn and a bottle of water.
A bag of popcorn costs twice as much as a bottle of water.
She receives \$3.55 change from \$10.
Work out the cost of a bag of popcorn and the cost of a bottle of water.

Bag of popcorn \AnswerLine[][\$]
Bottle of water \AnswerLine[][\$]
\Marks{3}

\item \begin{subparts}
\item A film lasts for 155 minutes.
Write 155 minutes in hours and minutes.

\AnswerLine[][hours] \AnswerLine[][minutes]
\Marks{1}

\item Another film starts at 14 45 and lasts for 2 hours and 20 minutes.
Work out the time that this film finishes.

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{1}

\end{subparts}

\item The table shows the hours that Trevor works in the cinema each week.

\relinput{Figures/Q01e.tex}

Monday

No hours

Tuesday

No hours

Wednesday

18 00 to 22 00

Thursday

18 00 to 22 00

Friday

12 00 to 17 30

Saturday

12 00 to 17 30

Sunday

13 00 to 18 00

Trevor is paid \$11.50 per hour for the time he works before 18 00.
He is paid 30\% more for the time he works after 18 00.
Work out how much Trevor is paid each week.

\AnswerLine[][\$]
\Marks{4}

\end{parts}

tex:
(a) 
Chen asks some people if they prefer a beach, cruise, lake or mountain holiday.
\newline

The pie chart shows the results.
\newline

(i)  Find the fraction of people who prefer a mountain holiday.
\newline

Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

 Find the percentage of people who prefer a beach holiday.
\newline

 Find the ratio of people who prefer each type of holiday in the form

beach : cruise : lake : mountain.
\newline
Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

 One person is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that this person prefers a cruise or a lake holiday.
\newline

 675 people prefer a beach holiday.
\newline

Show that the total number of people Chen asks is 1800.
\newline

\begin{subsubparts}

\subsubpart Complete the table.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
Type of holiday & Pie chart sector angle & Frequency \\
\hline
Beach & $135°$ & 675 \\
\hline
Cruise & $30°$ & 150 \\
\hline
Lake & $75°$ & \\
\hline
Mountain & $120°$ & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\subsubpart Complete the bar chart, including the scale on the frequency axis.
\newline

\end{subsubparts}

Mr Gibb pays \$2208 for a holiday.
\newline

Mr Shah pays 2050 euros for the same holiday.
\newline

The exchange rate is 1 euro = \$1.15.
\newline

Work out how much more, in euros, Mr Shah pays for the holiday than Mr Gibb.
\newline

content.content:
Chen asks some people if they prefer a beach, cruise, lake or mountain holiday.
\newline

Find the fraction of people who prefer a mountain holiday.
\newline

Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

Find the percentage of people who prefer a beach holiday.
\newline

Find the ratio of people who prefer each type of holiday in the form

\begin{center}
beach : cruise : lake : mountain.
\newline
\end{center}

Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

One person is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that this person prefers a cruise or a lake holiday.
\newline

675 people prefer a beach holiday.
\newline

Show that the total number of people Chen asks is 1800.
\newline
Complete the table.

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
Type of holiday & Pie chart sector angle & Frequency \\
\hline
Beach & $135\degree$ & 675 \\
\hline
Cruise & $30\degree$ & 150 \\
\hline
Lake & $75\degree$ & \\
\hline
Mountain & $120\degree$ & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
Complete the bar chart, including the scale on the frequency axis.

Mr Gibb pays \$2208 for a holiday.
\newline

Mr Shah pays 2050 euros for the same holiday.
\newline

The exchange rate is 1 euro = \$1.15.
\newline

Work out how much more, in euros, Mr Shah pays for the holiday than Mr Gibb.
\newline

tex:
(a) 
Here is part of the timetable for trains from Hinton to Jarmouth.
\newline

All trains take the same time to travel from Hinton to Jarmouth.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
Hinton & 10$\,$47 &  \\
\hline
Jarmouth & 11$\,$15 & 12$\,$35 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(i)  Complete the timetable.
\newline

 Marge arrives at Hinton station exactly 20 minutes before the 10$\,$47 train leaves.
\newline

Complete the clock diagram to show the time she arrives at Hinton station.
\newline

Each day, a bus leaves Texford to travel to Cranbrook every 45 minutes.
\newline

The first bus leaves Texford at 07$\,$10.
\newline

The last bus leaves Texford at 22$\,$10.
\newline

Work out the number of buses that travel from Texford to Cranbrook each day.
\newline

The cost of a bus pass increases every year.
\newline

On 1st January 2022 a bus pass costs \$50.
\newline

On 1st January 2023 the cost of the bus pass increases by 10\%.
\newline

On 1st January 2024 the cost of the bus pass increases by 5\%.
\newline

Calculate the cost of the bus pass on 1st January 2024.
\newline

The Venn diagram shows information about the number of workers in a hotel who travel to work by bus ($B$) and train ($T$).
\newline

(i)  Work out the number of workers in the hotel.
\newline

 Work out $n(B \cup T)$.
\newline

 Explain in words what the number 85 in the Venn diagram represents.
\newline

 One of the workers is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that this worker travels to work by bus and train.
\newline

The hotel has single and double bedrooms in the ratio single : double $= 3 : 8$.
\newline

There are 75 more double rooms than single rooms.
\newline

Work out the number of single rooms.
\newline

4 & 1   1   7 \\
5 & 0   7   7   8 \\
6 & 1 \\
\end{tabular}

Key: $2 \mid 5$ represents 25

tex:
(a) 
The scale drawing shows the position of a castle, $C$.
\newline

The scale is 1 centimetre represents 2 kilometres.
\newline

Micah walks at $4.8\,\text{km/h}$ for 3 hours on a bearing of $236°$ from $C$, to his house, $H$.
\newline

Mark the position of $H$ on the scale drawing.
\newline

A restaurant, $R$, is on a bearing of $083°$ from $C$.
\newline

(i)  Work out the bearing of $C$ from $R$.
\newline

 The distance, $d\,\text{km}$, from $R$ to $C$ is $5\,\text{km}$, correct to the nearest kilometre.
\newline

Complete this statement about the value of $d$.
\newline

...\ $\leqslant d <$ ...

A model of the castle is made.
\newline

On the model, the length of one of the castle walls is 5 centimetres.
\newline

The actual length of the wall is 90 metres.
\newline

Find the scale of the model in the form $1 : n$.
\newline

The castle has two kitchens with rectangular floors.
\newline

Rectangle $ABCD$ is mathematically similar to rectangle $EFGH$.
\newline

Calculate the value of $x$.
\newline

The castle has a cylindrical tower.
\newline

The tower has a radius of $2.4\,\text{m}$ and a height of $25\,\text{m}$.
\newline

Calculate the curved surface area of the tower.
\newline

Give the units of your answer.
\newline

tex:
The table shows some values for $y = x^2 - x - 3$.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
$x$ & $-3$ & $-2$ & $-1$ & $0$ & $1$ & $2$ & $3$ & $4$ \\
\hline
$y$ & & & $-1$ & & & $-1$ & & $9$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(a) 
Complete the table.
\newline

On the grid, draw the graph of $y = x^2 - x - 3$ for $-3 \leqslant x \leqslant 4$.
\newline

Write down the coordinates of the lowest point on the graph.
\newline

Use your graph to solve the equation $x^2 - x - 3 = 7$.
\newline
